mappings.rationale: Covers continental structure, oceanic crust, mountain ranges, and tectonic processes directly relevant to the physical features described throughout this section.
Directly addresses oceanic ridges (G), deep-sea trenches (D), subduction zones, mountain ranges (F), and volcanic island arcs as products of plate boundary interactions.
Covers the geophysical properties of continental shields (B.1), oceanic crust (D.1.a), sea-floor spreading (G), and techniques for studying subsurface structure (C.1).
Provides an overview of continental and oceanic physiography (A.2, A.3), continental drift, and the geological processes shaping the Earth's major surface features.
Covers oceanic physiography (A.3.b), continental margins (C), oceanic ridges (G), deep-sea trenches (D.1.b), abyssal plains, and seamounts.
Directly relevant to mountain types (F.1), the worldwide system of mountain ranges (F.2), and alpine valleys and landforms.
Covers volcanic mountains (F.1), volcanic island arcs (F.2.d), and the Circum-Pacific volcanic system (F.2.a).
Directly corresponds to section E on coral islands, coral reefs, and atolls.
Covers the physical landscape features including plateaus, rift valleys (B.2), coastal landforms, and the geomorphic context of the Earth's surface relief (A).
Relevant to the rock types constituting continental shields (B.1), folded mountain ranges (F.2.e), oceanic crust (D.1.a), and sediments of the ocean floor (D.1.b).
Relevant to the constituent rocks and minerals of continental shield areas (B.1) and the rock types in mountain ranges (F.2.e).
Directly relevant to water bodies occupying fault-bounded structural depressions (B.2.c), including lakes and landlocked seas.
Covers the physiography of Antarctica (A.2), its ice sheet, mountains, and surrounding oceanic features relevant to continental and oceanic description.
Covers Arctic continental shelf features (C), oceanic ridges in the Arctic basin, and the physical environment of the Arctic region (A.2, A.3).
Relevant to understanding glacially shaped continental physiography (A.2) and the relationship between glaciation and sea level affecting coastlines (A.1).
Covers ice sheets that shaped continental physiography (A.2) and their effects on sea level, coastlines (A.1), and continental shelf exposure (C).

mappings.vsiAuthor: John A. Matthews, David T. Herbert
mappings.rationale: Covers the physiography of continents (A.2), the distribution of land and sea (A.1), and the physical geography framework for understanding surface features.
